text:
IMPARTIAL NEWS + INTELLIGENT DEBATE

Account

If you thought athletics was dead after the 'strange' atmosphere in Tokyo, Paris 2024 is proving it's as relevant as ever

STADE DE FRANCE — The reports of athletics’ death have been grossly exaggerated, to paraphrase Mark Twain’s misquote.

It has been eight years since Olympic track-and-field athletes have been blessed by a capacity crowd to roar them on. But on Friday morning, on the opening day of action at the Stade de France, the noise was ferocious – for nothing more than sprint and middle-distance heats, decathlon rounds and qualifying for the men’s hammer throw and women’s high jump.

Back atRio 2016fans had a plethora of athletes to idolise, notablyUsain Bolt. But even with the star power of Bolt and the good-evil narrative between him and USA’sJustin Gatlin, and the intense rivalry between Shelly-Ann Fraser-Pryce and Tori Bowie in the women’s sprint events, the morning sessions at the track in Brazil were sparsely attended.

And sinceBolt’s retirement, athletics has been going through an existential crisis.Tokyo 2020was desperately sad, through no fault of the organisers, but because the pandemic precluded any spectators.

The apparent desperation of athletics to appear relevant is manifested in the Netflix seriesSprint, which is trying and only partially succeeding in conjuring some personalities out of the most prominent runners.

World champion Sha’Carri Richardson comes out best in it – but among the male stars, as cocky as Noah Lyles may be, he’s no Bolt.

But yesterday’s opening session on the gorgeous purple athletics track showed that fans still love a bit of athletics action. Whether this is because of or in spite of the exposure on Netflix is up for debate. But the hordes that cheered reigning world champion Richardson and her brightly coloured and claw-like fingernails in the opening 100m heat certainly know what they like.

Dina Asher-Smith, who safely made it to Saturday’s 100-metre semi-final by finishing second in her heat to Poland’s Ewa Swoboda, was impressed at the Parisian reception.

“It means a lot [to run in a full stadium],” the Briton said. “Tokyo was just strange for many reasons, and I think Paris with a full crowd, it’s really special.

“There are so many British fans in the crowd it really made my heart warm, and it adds to this amazing energy and atmosphere that the athletes can feel. We feed off this kind of energy.

“You’ve been in stadiums when it’s electric and that’s ultimately that’s what you want as a competitor, and it did feel really good this morning and feeling the cheers and excitement.”

A two-minute period towards the end of yesterday’s morning session personified the level of engagement shown by the 80,000-strong crowd. The eight runners in the final women’s 100m heat were going through their start routines. French decathlete Makenson Gletty was winding up for his shot-put throw.

The noise was deafening as he launched his shot. It grew louder still when Fraser-Pryce’s name was announced for the beginning of her fifth Games. Fraser-Pryce showed she is still a contender, two-and a-bit years before her 40thbirthday by qualifying joint third fastest for the semi-final, equal with Britain’s Daryll Neita.

Just as a reminder, this was for a heat. It was the same story for the men’s 1500m, in which Josh Kerr and Neil Gourley cantered to the semi-finals. There was one luminary involved in the heats – Norway’s Olympic record holder Jakob Ingebrigtsen, who is doubling up with the 5,000m at these Games.

Gourley, who ran in Ingebrigtsen’s heat and also qualified for Sunday’s semi-finals, said of the atmosphere: “It was something else. I didn’t take it all in until after the race to be honest. I had a wee look up and to see 80,000 people in the seats for a morning session of athletics was incredible.”

A full stadium is exactly what this sport needs and what these Games need. The morning oozed positivity (except from a British perspective for Morgan Lake, who missed out on qualification for the high jump final and former England footballer Danny Mills’ son George, who is in today’s repechage round for the 1500m).

There was no talk yesterday of athletics growing irrelevant in the face of Olympic upstarts like canoe cross and breakdancing – nor of the rival competition to the Diamond League instigated by US track legend Michael Johnson. The issues the sport has have not disappeared, but for a week or so we can put them aside.

And unlike Tokyo, we have a genuine star, one whose personality is strong and backstory is engaging, in Richardson.

She had to miss the last Games as she tested positive for cannabis after the US trials – but even if she had gone, the strangeness of the entire Games meant that there would have been minimal connection with fans. But she is here and means business.

The way she extended her wind-down at the end of her 100m run around the southern curve of the track to greet the crowd’s adulation hinted at more theatrics to come.

As the head of World Athletics Sebastian Coe said before the competition started: “The world is fractured and divided at the moment, but during the Olympics people come to watch jaw-dropping sport and the God-given talents of some of the most exceptional athletes that we have in any generation.”

Impartial news + intelligent debate

title: Athletics doesn't need fake hype or Netflix - it's still the main event
author: Matt Butler
description: If you thought athletics was dead after the 'strange' atmosphere in Tokyo, Paris 2024 is proving it's as relevant as ever
